1.synthesis:
The Philistines bring the captured ark to Ashdod, one of their five major cities, and set it in the temple of Dagon beside the statue of their god. The next morning they find Dagon fallen face down before the ark. They set him back in his place. The following morning, Dagon has again fallen before the ark — and this time his head and the palms of his hands have been cut off and are lying on the threshold. Only the trunk of Dagon remains. The narrator adds a dry etiological note: this is why the priests of Dagon and all who enter Dagon's temple do not step on the threshold at Ashdod to this day.

John Gill relishes the theological irony: Dagon falls prostrate before the ark of Israel's God — the very posture of worship. God did not send a prophet to confront the Philistines; he conducted his own defense through the humiliation of their idol. On the first morning, the fall could be attributed to accident; on the second, the decapitation removes all ambiguity. Dagon's posture on the second morning mimics the prostrate enemy before a conquering king, and the severing of his hands removes the symbol of his power and agency — he is powerless before the God of Israel even in the heart of his own temple.

Keil and Delitzsch observe that the narrative makes no mention of any human agent acting on Dagon — the idol falls and is mutilated by divine power alone, in the night, in the locked temple. This underscores that the ark's presence among the Philistines is not defeat but infiltration: God is operating in the enemy's stronghold, dismantling their religious confidence from within. Matthew Henry notes the pedagogical purpose: God shows the Philistines that they have not captured their God's superior; they have brought into their territory the sovereign Lord before whom every idol bows.
1.historical_context: Dagon was a major deity in the Philistine and earlier Canaanite-Semitic pantheons; Judg 16:23 depicts the Philistines sacrificing to Dagon in celebration of Samson's capture. Ashdod was one of the five Philistine city-states (along with Gaza, Gath, Ashkelon, and Ekron); the ark's circuit through Philistine cities in chapters 5–6 visits several of these. The etiological note about the threshold practice (5:5) is a common narrative feature in the Hebrew Bible: an existing cultural practice is given a narrative origin. The decapitation of the statue — head and hands severed — mirrors the treatment of defeated enemies in ancient Near Eastern warfare, where the conquering deity symbolically humiliated the gods of the vanquished.
1.literary_structure: The two-morning structure (fall alone; fall with decapitation) builds escalating intensity. Day one could be rationalized; day two cannot. The etiological note that closes the section functions as a narrative seal: the effects of this event are still visible in Philistine religious practice, which invites the reader to verify the story's reach into the present. The brevity of the narration — no dialogue, no human reaction until verse 6 — isolates God's action in the night as the exclusive agent.
1.theological_application: Calvin draws from Dagon's fall the doctrine of divine incomparability: the Lord alone is God, and beside him there is none. The Philistines have the ark but not the God of the ark; they discover that possessing the symbol of his presence is not control over him but exposure to him. For the church, this warns against idols of any kind — not only physical statues but the functional idols of security, success, or approval — being brought into the space God occupies. When the true God and a competing loyalty inhabit the same inner space, the idol will eventually fall. The threshold custom the Philistines maintained after this event is a permanent reminder embedded in their culture that the God of Israel is not like their gods — even the defeated symbol of his presence was enough to topple and dismember Dagon.
1.christology: The ark triumphing over Dagon in the heart of Philistine territory prefigures Christ's descent into death — the ultimate enemy territory — and his triumph there. Paul's citation of Ps 68:18 in Eph 4:8 — "When he ascended on high, he took many captives" — draws on the imagery of the victorious ark-God returning from the battle with spoils. As Dagon falls prostrate before the ark, Paul declares that at the name of Jesus "every knee shall bow, in heaven and on earth and under the earth" (Phil 2:10). The decapitation of Dagon's head anticipates the crushing of the serpent's head promised in Gen 3:15: the enemy of God's people, brought face to face with God's presence, is dismembered. Christ's resurrection is the ultimate Dagon-moment: death itself, the last enemy, is overthrown in the very place where it thought it held captive the Son of God.

6.synthesis:
The hand of the Lord is heavy on the Philistines of Ashdod: he afflicts them with tumors. The men of Ashdod recognize the connection between the ark and their suffering and send to the lords of the Philistines: "What shall we do with the ark of the God of Israel?" The decision is made to move it to Gath. The affliction follows the ark to Gath: the Lord's hand is against the city with a great panic, striking both small and great with the tumors. From Gath to Ekron: the Ekronites cry out that the ark has been brought to them to kill them and their people. The death-panic spreads through the city; those who don't die are struck with tumors. The ark of God is in Philistia for seven months.

The significance of the ark's circuit through three Philistine cities — Ashdod, Gath, Ekron — is that God's judgment is not confined to one place or one people. The Philistines cannot find a city willing to receive the ark that does not immediately experience its destructive power. They are discovering what the elders of Israel had hoped to manufacture: that the God of the ark is militarily effective against his enemies. But they experience this not from the perspective of Israel's ally but as his targets. Matthew Henry notes the bitter irony that the Philistines are experiencing the Exodus plagues in miniature — the God who struck Egypt is now striking them, with disease and panic, in exactly the same mode.

John Gill observes that the tumors striking "small and great" makes clear that this is not selective punishment of specific sinners but collective judgment on the entire community that participated in the capture and housing of the ark. Keil and Delitzsch emphasize the phrase "the hand of the Lord was very heavy" — the same language of divine weight used for the plagues of Egypt — which the narrative deploys to make the connection to Exodus explicit.
6.historical_context: The nature of the plague — tumors and mice — has suggested to many commentators a bubonic plague, in which swelling of the lymph nodes is characteristic and rats are vectors. The association of mice with the plague in the golden mice of chapter 6 supports this identification. The movement of the ark from city to city also resembles the spread of epidemic disease, though the narrator attributes it entirely to divine agency. The five lords of the Philistines (seranim) are the rulers of the five city-states; their consultative decision-making process is visible here and in chapter 6. The seven-month duration of the ark's captivity (6:1) before the Philistines return it suggests that they spent considerable time trying to manage the problem before concluding it was insurmountable.
6.literary_structure: The movement is a downward spiral for the Philistines: city one (Ashdod) is struck and passes the ark; city two (Gath) is struck and passes it; city three (Ekron) refuses to receive it but cannot prevent it and cries out in terror. Each iteration intensifies: tumors in Ashdod, great panic and tumors in Gath, death-panic and tumors in Ekron. The accumulation makes clear that there is no Philistine location that can contain the ark safely. The chapter closes with an abrupt time-notation — seven months — that signals the end of one phase and the beginning of negotiations for return.
6.theological_application: Calvin reads the Philistine experience as a demonstration that God's sovereign judgment cannot be geographically managed. Moving the problem to a neighboring city does not solve it; it spreads it. This applies to any attempt to keep one's sins contained rather than confessing and relinquishing them: the plague follows. The collective nature of the judgment — "small and great" alike — raises the question of communal responsibility, which Matthew Henry addresses: when a community harbors what belongs to God without acknowledgment or repentance, the entire community bears the weight of that sin, not merely the leaders who made the decision. The Philistines' eventual decision to return the ark is not repentance but pragmatism — they want the affliction to stop. Yet even this pragmatic response involves a degree of theological reasoning (chapter 6) that God honors by ending the plague.
6.christology: The ark's progress through Philistia, bringing judgment to every city that houses it without proper acknowledgment, prefigures the advent of the Gospel into the world, which is "to some a fragrance from death to death, to others a fragrance from life to life" (2 Cor 2:16). The presence of Christ, like the presence of the ark, is not neutral: it judges as well as saves. The Philistines who house the ark without repentance experience it as destruction; Israel, when it is properly restored, will experience it as salvation. God's presence is the same in both cases; the difference is in the posture of the recipient.
